title: The race for an Ebola therapy begins
summary: This STAT Morning Rounds roundup covers multiple health stories, including early efforts to develop new Ebola therapeutics amid the ongoing search for effective treatments. The newsletter also highlights GOP lawmakers working to improve clinical trial diversity and coverage of male marathoners' mental health challenges.
analysis_notes: Paywall blocked full article body. Only title, bylines, newsletter description, and sidebar headlines visible. Content treated as a brief roundup/Newsletter piece rather than an in-depth investigative article. Entities inferred from context clues (Morning Rounds authors include infectious disease correspondent Helen Branswell).

title: Pharmalittle: Novartis Buy of Myricx Bio, GOP Backs Clinical Trial Diversity — And More
summary: Novartis is paying $1.1 billion upfront to acquire London-based Myricx Bio, a deal that accelerates Novartis' entry into the antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) space using a novel N-myristoyltransferase inhibitor (NMTi) payload platform designed to overcome resistance and expand ADC use across tumor types. Separately, Republican lawmakers in the House-backed funding bill urge the FDA to maintain requirements that drug companies submit clinical trial diversity plans, even as the Trump administration has weakened related DEI policies. The newsletter also leads with the first-ever clinical trial testing treatments against the Ebola Bundibugyo virus.
analysis_notes: Content is from a newsletter roundup with one article fully behind the paywall. Summary drawn from article preamble and open excerpts only; key details on other roundup items (Ebola Bundibugyo trial, clinical trial diversity tie-in) are inferred from headlines and section headers visible in the page. The $1.1B Myricx acquisition is confirmed via an external Fierce Biotech link.

title: Four major biotech updates to catch up on
summary: Novartis agreed to acquire U.K.-based Myricx Bio for $1.1 billion upfront plus up to $400 million in milestones, gaining an antibody-drug conjugate platform targeting solid tumors that overcomes limitations of existing ADC technologies. Anthropics CEO Dario Amodei tempered expectations about AI transforming biotechnology, acknowledging biology's inherent complexity and long timelines despite AIs potential to compress scientific progress.
analysis_notes: Article is partially behind STAT+ paywall. Only two of four headline topics received substantive coverage in available text: Novartis-Myricx acquisition and Anthropic CEO interview. Medicare 340B payment cuts, GOP clinical trial diversity legislation, and peptides regulation by former Surgeon General Jerome Adams were mentioned in lede only with no extractable detail.

title: Obamacare rolls shrank dramatically in many states over the past year, new federal data shows
summary: Federal data reveals approximately 2.6 million fewer Americans were enrolled in ACA marketplace plans in February 2026 compared to February 2025, following the January expiration of enhanced subsidy amounts. Ohio and Oklahoma experienced the steepest declines, each losing nearly one-third of their enrollees, according to the first complete 50-state breakdown released by the Trump administration in late June.

title: STAT Plus: For a Georgia entrepreneur, health coverage has never been affordable
summary: Amy Bielawski, a 61-year-old small business owner of an entertainment company in Tucker, Ga., has struggled with ACA marketplace health insurance as out-of-pocket costs have become unaffordable. The article profiles her case within STAT's broader Out of Pocket, Out of Reach series, which examines how rising healthcare costs are reshaping labor markets and forcing solo business owners without employer-sponsored coverage to fend for themselves.
analysis_notes: Full article body was behind a STAT+ paywall. Summary derived from article headline, series description, caption text describing Amy Bielawski's case, and related article recommendations. The Out of Pocket, Out of Reach series (Part 1 published same date) appears to be a major investigative effort on small business health insurance affordability.

title: What if this Ebola outbreak can't be stopped?
summary: Authors Ivan Buendia Gayton and Eric D. Perakslis warn that the worst-case scenario for the current Ebola outbreak in eastern DRC is not that it spreads outward, but that it becomes endemic and persists indefinitely. Drawing on their firsthand experience during the 2014–2016 West Africa epidemic and the 2018–2020 DRC outbreaks, they argue that the extraordinary international cooperation, local adaptations, and luck that ended the earlier epidemics are largely absent today. The article frames ongoing Ebola transmission in the DRC as a chronic, decades-long threat to global health.

title: This whistleblower took on a health insurance giant and a political machine. She's not stopping there
summary: Chris Deacon, an attorney and health insurance consultant, was instrumental in uncovering fraud at Horizon Blue Cross Blue Shield that led to a $100 million settlement against the insurer for knowingly overpaying hospitals and doctors and fraudulently winning its contract to manage New Jersey's state health plan covering 750,000 workers. Despite her central role, Deacon was angered by how state officials erased her contributions at the press conference announcing the settlement, fueling her ongoing crusade against industry profiteering in health care.

entities.name: STAT Out of Pocket, Out of Reach series

analysis_notes: Full article text is behind STAT+ paywall. Summary constructed from headline, lead paragraphs, photo caption, and topic tags extracted before the paywall wall. Core narrative about the Horizon BCBS fraud settlement and whistleblower Chris Deacon is present in the accessible content.

title: Opinion: Medicine thinks Gen Z is too soft. It’s wrong
summary: By Frantz M. Berthaud, SVP of oncology services at University Medical Center of El Paso, this opinion piece argues against the stereotype that Gen Z physicians are too soft for medicine. Framed around a personal anecdote about visiting an unusually young doctor who turned out to be competent and personable, the article contends that health care executives should listen to younger doctors rather than dismiss them, as the workforce needs their contributions not their elimination.

entities.name: University Medical Center of El Paso

title: America's small businesses are giving up on health insurance
summary: U.S. small businesses are offering employer-based health insurance to workers at historically low levels, with the share of working-age adults covered through employment falling from 67% in 1998 to about 60%. Soaring premiums driven by expensive hospitals, doctors, and prescription drugs are forcing desperate companies to eliminate traditional health benefits at an unprecedented pace. The article is Part 1 of STAT's 'Out of Pocket, Out of Reach' series, based on interviews with over 50 small business owners, workers, brokers, consultants, and insurance executives.
